title: Chapter 26: Undressing
content: Three weeks of hand-holding.

Three weeks of walks along the river path where the willows dragged their fingers through the current and Julian's thumb traced slow circles on the inside of her wrist. Three weeks of shared margins, of passages read aloud in the amber light of his apartment, her voice and his finding the same cadence like two instruments tuning to each other. Three weeks of slow reading—not just texts, but each other. The way he pressed his lips together before making a counterargument. The way she tucked her hair behind her left ear when she was nervous and behind her right when she was thinking.
Three weeks in which Maeve Quinn had been more alive than she had ever been.
And still: the wall.

The body was the wall. She could feel it at night, lying in her narrow bed in Hartwell Hall, listening to Chloe's breathing across the room. She could feel it in the morning when she stood in front of the mirror and performed the ritual—layering, adjusting, covering. She could feel it every time Julian's hand found hers and she let it stay there, wanting to pull him closer and terrified of what closer would mean.
Because closer meant seeing. And seeing meant the body. And the body was the wall.

Julian had been patient beyond reason. He hadn't pushed. He hadn't pressured. He hadn't even hinted in that careful, testing way that people do when they want something but don't want to seem like they want it. When they sat together on his sofa, he kept a respectful distance—close enough that she could feel the warmth of him, never so close that she felt crowded. When they kissed, which they did now with growing frequency and duration, his hands stayed in safe territory: her jaw, her hair, the curve of her shoulder over her shirt. He explored her the way one explores a rare book—carefully, with reverence, never forcing the spine.

But she could see it in the way he looked at her sometimes. When she emerged from his bathroom with her hair still damp from the rain. When she stretched her arms above her head after too long bent over a text. When she laughed—really laughed, the kind that shook her shoulders and made her close her eyes—and for a moment she forgot to hold herself in.

Wanting. Not desperation. Patience. The patience of a man who knows what he's waiting for and is willing to wait. And that patience, somehow, was worse than pressure would have been. Because pressure she could resist. Pressure was a wall she could press back against. But his patience was an open door, and she was the one standing on the threshold, unable to step through.

It was a Friday in late March. The kind of evening that couldn't decide if it was winter's last gasp or spring's first breath—cold enough for coats, but the light had that golden, stretching quality that meant the days were lengthening. They'd been in his apartment since late afternoon, working through Derrida's "Signature Event Context" with the grim determination of people who suspected the text was laughing at them.

The wine had helped. Two glasses each, a rough Malbec that stained the inside of the glass purple and left Maeve's lips feeling fruit-dark and warm. Julian read aloud in his careful, slightly lisping French, and she followed along in her English translation, and they argued about whether "communication" was even possible or whether every utterance was essentially a letter sent to an address that no longer existed.

"You're being too generous," she said, reaching for the bottle to pour a third glass. "Derrida isn't saying communication fails. He's saying the concept of communication presupposes a presence that was never there to begin with."
"And I'm saying that's the same thing dressed up in fancier language."
"It's not the same thing. Failing implies something was supposed to succeed. Derrida's point is that the entire framework of success and failure is—"
She reached.

The book she wanted—Derrida's *Limited Inc*, which she'd been using to cross-reference—was on the high shelf above his desk. She stood on her toes, stretching, and felt the hem of her cardigan ride up, and felt his hand on the small of her back.
Just that. His palm flat against her spine, steadying her. The touch lasted maybe three seconds. He reached past her with his other hand and retrieved the book, and then the hand was gone.
But she felt it for the next twenty minutes.

She felt it like a brand through the cotton of her T-shirt, like a sunburn that pulsed with her heartbeat. His hand. The weight and warmth of it. The casualness—the way he'd done it without thinking, without asking, because it was what you did when someone reached for something and you were close enough to help. But it hadn't been casual for her. It had been a door opening onto a room she'd spent her whole life keeping locked.
*I want more.*

The thought arrived complete and uninvited, like a guest at a party who wasn't on the list but somehow got past the door. She looked at him across the sofa, at his hands holding the book, at the way the lamplight caught the edge of his jaw, and the thought clarified itself with brutal precision: *I want him to touch me and not be disappointed.*

There it was. The fear, wearing its usual mask. Disappointment. She'd imagined it a thousand times—rehearsed it, really, the way one rehearses for a disaster that feels inevitable. His face when he saw her body. Not disgust; she didn't think he was capable of disgust, not in that way. But something. A subtle flicker of… recalibration. The moment when the mind he loved met the body she hated, and the two images failed to reconcile. The slight widening of the eyes. The pause before he spoke. The careful, measured tenderness that would be worse than rejection because it would be kindness costumed as desire.

She'd seen it before. Not from him—from the world. From mirrors and dressing rooms and the quiet arithmetic of other people's glances. She knew what her body was. She knew what it wasn't. And she knew that no amount of intellectual connection could inoculate against the shock of the physical.
But she was tired. God, she was so tired of the wall.
"Maeve?" Julian's voice cut through. "You've been staring at the same page for about two minutes. Even by Derrida standards, that's excessive."
She looked up. He was watching her with that look—half-amused, half-concerned, wholly present. The look that said *I see you, even when you're trying not to be seen.*
She stood up from the sofa.
He looked at her, questioning. "Everything okay?"
"I need to show you something."

Her voice was shaking. She heard it and couldn't stop it—the tremor running through the words like a crack through glass. She wasn't talking about a text or a scar or a metaphor. She wasn't talking about the garden or the letters or any of the things she'd already given him. She was talking about the thing she'd hidden her entire adult life. The thing no one had ever seen and responded to with anything other than the flinch she'd already anticipated.

Julian set the book down slowly. "Okay," he said. No impatience. No anticipation. Just *okay*, the way you say it when someone tells you they need to tell you something and you understand, without being told, that it matters.

She took off her cardigan. Her fingers were clumsy on the buttons—two of them, the second one stubborn, her hands trembling so badly she had to try twice. She folded it and set it on the arm of the sofa, a gesture of delay so transparent she almost laughed. Underneath was a plain black T-shirt. Old. Soft from washing. The kind of shirt that disappears into the background, which was, of course, the point.
She pulled it over her head.

She was not looking at him. She couldn't look at him—her eyes were fixed on the ceiling, on the water stain in the corner that looked like a continent, on anything that wasn't his face. She heard the soft sound the shirt made as it left her skin, felt the air of the room hit her shoulders, her stomach, the exposed skin above the waistband of her jeans. She was standing in his apartment in her bra and jeans, arms at her sides, fists clenched so hard her nails were cutting into her palms. She was not breathing. She had forgotten how.

This was the most terrifying thing she'd ever done. Worse than the garden—because the garden was a story, and she could shape stories. Worse than the letters—because letters were words, and she had always been able to trust words. This was her body, and there was no text to hide behind. No metaphor. No eloquent framing. Just flesh and bone and the brutal honesty of skin.

The apartment was silent. She could hear the old radiator clicking in the corner, could hear traffic on the street below, could smell the Malbec and old paper and the faint cedar of his cologne. She could feel the cool air on her bare midriff, could taste the wine still on her lips, could see—still—the continent-shaped stain on the ceiling. Every sense was acute, heightened, screaming.
His voice, when it came, was rough. Broken at the edges. "Maeve."
She shook her head. She couldn't look. If she looked and saw the thing she'd been dreading, she would not survive it.
"Look at me," he said.
She shook her head again, harder this time, her hair swinging against her bare shoulders.

She heard him stand. The creak of the sofa. Footsteps on the hardwood—two steps, three—close now. She could feel the heat of him without touching, the warmth of his body radiating toward hers. The cedar-and-paper smell of him stronger now, closer. He didn't touch her. He stood close enough that she could feel his presence like a change in weather, and he didn't touch her.

"I'm going to tell you what I see," he said. His voice was low and steady, but there was something underneath it—a vibration, like a string pulled taut. "And I need you to hear me. Not the fear in your head. Me."
A breath. She could hear it—the small, controlled inhale of a man steadying himself.

"I see you. For the first time. All of you." Another breath. "And you are…" The pause stretched. It stretched past the radiator's click, past the traffic, past the continent on the ceiling. It stretched into what felt like the entire history of her fear—the years of covering and hiding and believing, with the certainty of bone, that this moment would confirm everything she'd always known about herself.

"You are so much more than I imagined. And I imagined a lot." His voice caught—actually caught, like a record skipping, and he had to start again. "Because I've been falling in love with you since September, Maeve. Since the first letter. Since the first margin note in that goddamn Dante. And every time I learned something new about you—the way you argue when you're scared, the way you hum when you read, the way you look at the rain like it personally offended you—the picture got more complete. Every detail was another brushstroke. And now I see all of it. All at once. And it's…"
His voice cracked. Not a small crack—a fracture that went all the way through, and she heard him swipe at his face with the back of his hand, though she still couldn't look.
"It's not less, Maeve. It's more. It's so much more."
She opened her eyes.

He was standing in front of her—close enough that she could see the lamplight in his eyes, the gold flecks she'd never noticed before. And his face was not pity. Not tolerance. Not the careful neutrality of someone managing disappointment, choosing words to minimize damage. It was wonder. The same wonder she'd heard in his voice the night he'd first read Rilke aloud to her, when he'd spoken about the *Archaic Torso of Apollo* and the line that changed his life—*You must change your life*—and his whole being had seemed lit from within by the presence of something beautiful and true.

He was looking at her the way he looked at poems. Like she was a text he'd been studying in translation for months and was finally reading in the original language, and the original was better than any translation could have prepared him for.
She whispered: "You're not disappointed?"
And he said it. The line that would undo her—the line that would rewrite, in a single breath, every assumption she'd ever made about what she deserved:

"I fell in love with you before I knew what you looked like. I kissed your mind for six months." His voice was rough and raw and absolutely steady. "Do you really think a few superficial inches of skin could make me question the person who wrote me back to life?"
She broke.

Not into tears—not this time. Into laughter. Wild, unstoppable, slightly hysterical laughter that started somewhere below her ribs and surged up through her chest and out of her mouth in a sound she didn't recognize. She was laughing and she couldn't stop, and her bare shoulders were shaking, and the tears were coming now too, but they were tangled up with the laughter, and she was standing in his apartment half-dressed and laughing like a woman who had just heard the funniest joke in the history of the world.

Which, in a way, she had. The joke was on her. The joke was that she'd spent twenty-two years terrified that her body was the thing that would disqualify her from love, and the man standing in front of her had never been looking at bodies in the first place. He'd been looking for a mind. And she'd given him hers—freely, fully, without reservation—in the margins of books and the space between letters and the long, stumbling, honest conversations that had built them from strangers into this.

This impossible man. This impossible, patient, maddening man who had stood on the other side of the wall and waited—not because he didn't want to climb it, but because he understood that she needed to be the one to open the gate.
Her laughter subsided. It drained out of her slowly, leaving something quiet and clean in its wake. She wiped her eyes with the back of her hand and finally—*finally*—met his gaze and held it.

He was smiling. Not the tight, reassuring smile of someone trying to make her feel better. A real smile, crooked and wondering, the smile of a man who had just watched something extraordinary and didn't quite have the language for it yet. He bent down and picked up her T-shirt from the floor where it had fallen, and he held it out to her.
"Only if you want," he said.

She took the shirt from his hands. Their fingers brushed, and the touch was electric—not charged with wanting, not this time, but with something quieter. Recognition. The meeting of two people who had just crossed a threshold together and were standing on the other side, blinking in the unfamiliar light.
She didn't put the shirt on.

She held it against her stomach, her arms crossed over it, and she let him see her. Really see her. Not the version she performed in layers and angles and strategic draping, but the body as it was—soft and imperfect and exposed, standing in the warm light of his apartment with her hair tangled and her eyes red and her heart so raw it felt like an open wound that was, for the first time, beginning to close.
One long moment. An eternity in an eye-blink. She held his gaze, and he held hers, and the silence between them was the most articulate thing she'd ever heard.

Then she pulled the shirt over her head. The fabric slid down over her stomach, her shoulders, her arms, and she was covered again, but different. The wall was still there—she could feel it, solid and familiar, a structure built over decades and not dismantled by a single evening. But there was a door in it now. She'd opened it. And on the other side was Julian, who was not the enemy she'd always imagined but just a man who had been waiting, with infinite patience, for her to realize he was there.

He stepped forward and put his arms around her. Not tentatively—fully, completely, pulling her against his chest with a sureness that made her breath catch. She could feel his heartbeat against her cheek, steady as a metronome, and the rough weave of his sweater, and the warmth of his hands spread wide across her back, pressing her into him like he was trying to make sure she knew—as surely as he'd said it—that she was not too much. Not too little. Just this. Just her.
She leaned into him.
And for the first time in her adult life, being inside her body didn't feel like a punishment.
